items.title: Gianni Infantino under fire as viral claims drag European Parliament into FIFA row
items.description: Viral claims with millions of views falsely claim. the European Parliament has launched a probe into FIFA chief Gianni Infantino. In reality, the move came from a letter by 72 MEPs to EU football associations, urging them to seek a formal FIFA review into the controversial Balogun red card reversal. No probe has been opened, but the episode adds to growing scrutiny around FIFA’s president.

items.title: War, talks, repeat: How to break the cycle between US and Iran?

items.title: Spain's 'Formidable Opponent': Rajoy credits France's football success to having 'no French players'
items.description: Nadia Massih is pleased to welcome Dr Leon Moosavi, Sociologist of race and religion, and Reader in the Department of Sociology, Social Policy and Criminology at the University of Liverpool. Racism directed at the French national football team is often framed as a problem of sport, yet Dr. Moosavi argues that football merely provides a highly visible stage upon which much deeper political and social conflicts play out. Rather than viewing racist abuse against players such as Kylian Mbappé as isolated incidents or the actions of a few extremists, our guest describes these attacks within wider debates over national identity, immigration, belonging, and the normalization of extremist rhetoric and exclusionary political discourse across many Western democracies.

items.title: How people’s genes are revealing why some languages are so unique
items.description: A new study measured how different neighbouring languages were from one another. The authors say that linguistic hotspots may offer glimpses into how human languages became more varied before large migrations and population movements reshaped much of the world

items.title: Collapse of Matlala plea deal complicates South Africa's police corruption case
items.description: The failed plea bargain is seen as a significant blow to South Africa's anti-corruption campaign, as Matlala's testimony was expected to help expose alleged corruption within the country's police service.

items.title: Gas queues grow as Ukraine targets Russia's fuel supply
items.description: Attacks by Ukraine have triggered a fuel crisis in Russia. Reports from across Russia show how residents are dealing with the disruptions in their daily lives.

items.title: US military reimposes naval blockade on Iranian ports, launches new strikes
items.description: US forces on Tuesday reimposed a naval blockade to prevent ships from sailing to or from Iran's ports and conducted strikes against the country for a fourth day in response to Tehran's attacks on commercial ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz and on Gulf facilities.

UK police arrest murder suspect in Ann Widdecombe's death
items.description: Police in southwest England say they have arrested a 26-year-old man on suspicion of murdering politician Ann Widdecombe. Based on initial inquiries, they were not treating the case as terrorism or politically motivated.

items.title: People flee Bangkok bar as huge flames shoot out from doorway
items.description: At least 28 people have died and 25 are critically injured after a fire tore through a bar in a popular Bangkok district.

items.title: France marks tenth anniversary of deadly Nice terror attack
items.description: President Emmanuel Macron held a national tribute in Nice on Tuesday for the victims of a deadly jihadist attack 10 years ago in the southern French city. Later Tuesday, the French football team will observe a minute of silence ahead of their World Cup semi-final in a commemoration to the 86 victims of the attack.

items.title: China expels senior Communist party leader in anti-corruption crackdown
items.description: Ma Xingrui is the third member of the 24-member Politburo to come under investigation during the current party term that began in 2022, a rare occurrence in the recent history of the 105-year-old party

items.title: Uganda: Court clears way for Besigye treason trial
items.description: The High Court has dismissed an application by Dr. Kizza Besigye and Hajji Obeid Lutale seeking to terminate treason proceedings, clearing the way for their trial to begin on July 13, 2026. The court ruled the application lacked merit and was intended to delay the criminal case.
